channel.title: NYT > Books > Book Review
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Ending Writes Itself,’ by Evelyn Clarke
item.description: In this novel, a group of authors race to finish a mystery manuscript, only to find themselves part of a lethal plot.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Harlan

item.title: Book Review: ‘American Fantasy,’ by Emma Straub
item.description: In Emma Straub’s latest novel, “American Fantasy,” a pop group’s midlife return provides fodder for both comedy and redemption on the high seas.
item.dc:creator: Leah Greenblatt

item.title: Book Review: ‘Corto Maltese,’ by Hugo Pratt
item.description: “Corto Maltese,” Hugo Pratt’s influential 1967 graphic novel, returns, with just as much to say about childhood during wartime.
item.dc:creator: Sam Thielman
item.category: Corto Maltese: Fable of Venice and Other Adventures (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘Yesteryear,’ by Caro Claire Burke
item.description: In Caro Claire Burke’s novel, “Yesteryear,” a homesteading momfluencer can no longer hide the scandal swirling just below the surface.
item.dc:creator: Michelle Ruiz

item.title: The 10 Best Books of 2025
item.description: The staff of The New York Times Book Review choose the year’s top fiction and nonfiction.
item.category: The Loneliness of Sonia and Sunny (Book)
There Is No Place for Us: Working and Homeless in America (Book)
Mother Emanuel: Two Centuries of Race, Resistance, and Forgiveness in One Charleston Church (Book)
A Marriage at Sea: A True Story of Love, Obsession, and Shipwreck (Book)
Wild Thing: A Life of Paul Gauguin (Book)

item.title: Fanciful and Fabulous: ‘Cats: The Jellicle Ball’ on Broadway
item.description: For their 10th life, the cats strut and duckwalk in a reappraisal of Andrew Lloyd Webber’s 1981 musical, which has shifted to the queer ballroom scene.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw and Sara Krulwich

item.title: Norman Bussel, Who Chronicled Veterans’ Unseen Traumas, Dies at 102
item.description: After a grueling year in a German prison camp during World War II, he endured crushing nightmares and survivor guilt back home, leading him to spread the word about veterans’ suffering.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen

item.title: Peter Schrag Dies at 94; Wrote of Dangers of California’s Populist Streak
item.description: His best-received book explored the state’s infatuation with voter initiatives, which were sometimes pushed with anti-immigrant fervor.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel
item.category: Paradise Lost: California's Experience, America's Future (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘Hexes of the Deadwood Forest,’ by Agnieszka Szpila
item.description: The Polish best seller “Hexes of the Deadwood Forest” is like a post-porn fever dream of Eastern European magic realism crossed with a plant-based “Joy of Sex.”
item.dc:creator: Sadie Stein

item.title: How Did Black Music Take Over the World? Let Melvin Gibbs Explain.
item.description: Since the late ’70s, the bassist has worked to map a musical route that mirrored the trans-Atlantic slave trade and birthed nearly all of American popular music.
item.dc:creator: Hank Shteamer
item.category: How Black Music Took Over the World (Book)
item.media:credit: Galen Fletcher for The New York Times
item.media:description: Melvin Gibbs has become one of his generation’s most admired and least easily categorized instrumentalists and conceptualists.

item.title: Ronald H. Spector, Who Traced Social History in Books on War, Dies at 83
item.description: A Vietnam veteran-turned-academic historian, he drew acclaim for portraying conflicts from the perspectives of generals as well as grunts on all sides, both in Vietnam and in World War II.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Oyster Diaries,’ by Nancy Lemann
item.description: The well-born protagonist of Nancy Lemann’s novel “The Oyster Diaries” returns home and immediately feels like an outsider.
item.dc:creator: Dwight Garner
item.media:description: Nancy Lemann writes about women who are frank, sardonic, bookish and neurotic. She channels their discontent into slashing little thunderstorms of meaning, our critic writes.

item.title: Book Review: ‘Here Where We Live Is Our Country,’ by Molly Crabapple
item.description: In “Here Where We Live Is Our Country,” Molly Crabapple tells the story of a Jewish labor movement that fought antisemitism and nationalism with equal fervor.
item.dc:creator: Max Strasser
item.category: Here Where We Live Is Our Country: The Story of the Jewish Bund (Book)

item.title: Great Police Procedural Novels
item.description: These novels marry good mysteries with unforgettable characters and the twists and turns of the investigative process to deliver page-turning thrills.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Weinman

item.title: How Scientifically Accurate Is ‘Project Hail Mary’? Experts Weigh In
item.description: Based on hard science fiction, a genre that prioritizes scientific accuracy, the blockbuster gets a lot right but misses a few things, experts say.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski

item.title: The Month’s Best New Thriller Books
item.description: Our columnist on the month’s best new releases.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Lyall

item.title: Book Review: ‘True Color,’ by Kory Stamper
item.description: The lexicographer Kory Stamper’s “True Color” is a sneakily insightful philosophical treatise on what it means to define anything at all.
item.dc:creator: Deirdre Mask

item.title: 23 Books We Are Looking Forward to This Spring
item.description: The Book Review editors discuss fiction and nonfiction that caught their eye. Plus, Ada Limón on the power of poetry.
item.dc:creator: Gilbert Cruz, Sarah Diamond, Amy Pearl and Larissa Anderson
item.media:credit: Illustration by The New York Times; Inset photos: Scribner; Viking; Spiegel & Grau

item.title: What to Know About ‘The Testaments,’ a Sequel to Margaret Atwood’s ‘The Handmaid’s Tale’
item.description: “The Testaments” focuses on a younger generation coming of age inside Gilead, the religious regime first imagined in Margaret Atwood’s 1985 dystopian thriller.
item.dc:creator: Martine Thompson
item.category: The Testaments: The Sequel to The Handmaid's Tale (Book)

item.title: Ben Lerner’s ‘Transcription’ Shows Us Why the Novel Will Never Die
item.description: With “Transcription,” the writer makes a case for the vitality of the form.
item.dc:creator: Parul Sehgal

item.title: Book Review: ‘Judgy Bunny and the Terrible Beach,’ by Scott Rothman, and ‘Bartleby,’ by Matt Phelan
item.description: Matt Phelan’s bear cub named Bartleby and Scott Rothman’s judgy bunny aren’t wicked or misbehaved. Like our reviewer, they simply prefer not to.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Brown
item.category: Judgy Bunny and the Terrible Beach (Book)

item.title: Books Our Editors Loved This Week
item.description: Reading recommendations from critics and editors at The New York Times.

item.title: Meet the ‘Literary King of Tulsa’ (Before He Moves to Seattle)
item.description: In his free time, Jeff Martin mobilized best-selling authors to travel to sold-out events in his hometown. He will soon expand his horizons.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Egan
item.media:credit: September Dawn Bottoms for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jeff Martin opened the independent bookstore Magic City Books in Tulsa in 2017. Since then, it’s become a bastion of bibliophilia in the midsize prairie town.

item.title: Terry Tempest Williams on Thoreau, Erdrich and Other Favorite Writers
item.description: She vividly recalls what the novel, and others like it, meant to her mother. Her own new book is “The Glorians: Visitations From the Holy Ordinary.”
item.category: The Glorians: Visitations From the Holy Ordinary (Book)

item.title: Great Chapter Books for Beginning Readers
item.description: Here are some of our staff’s favorites, for ages 4 to 8.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times Books Staff
item.category: Brand-New School, Brave New Ruby (Ruby and the Booker Boys, Book 1) (Book)
Ling & Ting: Not Exactly the Same! (Book)
Lost Treasure of the Emerald Eye (Book)
Henry and Mudge: The First Book (Book)
Junie B. Jones and the Stupid Smelly Bus (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘This Land Is Your Land,’ by Beverly Gage
item.description: In anticipation of the nation’s 250th anniversary, a Pulitzer winner visited 300 sites to see how history is displayed and, sometimes, erased.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Szalai
item.category: This Land Is Your Land: A Road Trip Through US History (Book)
item.media:description: The Confederate flag flies at Fort Sumter, S.C., at the beginning of the Civil War, in April 1861.

item.title: 10 Short Books for Spring
item.description: These single-serving satires, family dramas and romances can be read cover-to-cover in one sun-dappled afternoon.
item.dc:creator: Calum Marsh

item.title: 16 Strangers, One 304-Page Novel and a Weekend of Reading Aloud
item.description: What happens when you shrink down a book club to two days and take turns narrating the story? Welcome to Page Break.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Egan and Yuvraj Khanna
item.media:description: From left: Victoria Cuenca, Max Pittman and Mikey Friedman, the founder of Page Break.
